remission.spontaneous_remission_rate: Structural progression is essentially universal over 10+ years without intervention; pain fluctuates but functional decline is the expected trajectory; no disease-modifying therapy exists as of 2024
remission.best_intervention_remission_rate: No cure or disease modification available; total joint replacement (TJR) achieves 85-90% patient satisfaction and near-elimination of pain but is not disease reversal; weight loss of >10% reduces pain scores ~50% and slows structural progression; exercise therapy comparable to NSAIDs for pain relief
remission.gap_size: Large for pain management vs disease modification (disease modification doesn't exist); moderate for functional outcomes (exercise and weight loss underutilized vs medication)
remission.barrier_detail: OARSI formally petitioned the FDA in 2016 to recognize OA as a serious disease with no cure and no disease-modifying interventions — confirmed in FDA's criteria. Practice gap: NSAIDs have been first-line for >100 years despite long-term evidence of joint damage acceleration and GI/CV risk; exercise consistently outperforms medication in functional outcomes but is rarely 'prescribed'
remission.last_soc_change: 2019-2020 — OARSI/ACR guidelines formally de-emphasized acetaminophen (evidence of minimal efficacy) and added conditional recommendation against opioids; NSAIDs have been first-line since the 1960s; duloxetine added ~2010 as centrally-acting option
remission.outdated_treatments: Long-term oral NSAID use (4-year MRI data shows NSAIDs may accelerate cartilage loss; GI bleed and CV risk substantial in elderly OA population); intra-articular corticosteroid injections repeated >3x/year (joint damage acceleration); opioids for chronic OA pain (mortality signal in observational data); acetaminophen (not meaningfully better than placebo in recent RCTs for OA pain)
remission.adjunctive_emerging: Platelet-rich plasma (PRP) injections — multiple RCTs showing 3-6 month pain reduction; low-level laser therapy (LLLT); hydrogen sulfide donors (early animal data); senolytic therapy (dasatinib + quercetin — first human OA trial published 2019 showing reduced senescent cells)
remission.frontier_signal: Lorecivivint (Wnt pathway inhibitor, Phase 3 failed 2022 but variant study ongoing); sprifermin (FGF-18, cartilage anabolic, Phase 2 positive); anti-nerve growth factor (tanezumab — FDA rejected over joint safety, remains contested); gene therapy (AAV-based IGF-1 delivery to joint)
remission.notes: Manually curated from landmark trials, registries, and guidelines (disease_db_100.json). Protected from automated overwrite.
